questions.text: Which MST protocol provides a distributed, verifiable randomness beacon for all games?

options.text: FortunaX
Klethesia
SatvaShuffle
WASMify

questions.explanation: FortunaX is MST's distributed randomness beacon, using threshold BLS signatures from Trika POSA validators to produce unpredictable, unbiasable random numbers.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

FortunaX is MST's distributed randomness beacon, using threshold BLS signatures from Trika POSA validators to produce unpredictable, unbiasable random numbers.

questions.text: SatvaShuffle guarantees a fair card shuffle by using which cryptographic technique?

options.text: A ZK-SNARK proof that the shuffled deck is a valid permutation of the standard deck
A multi-party computation among all players
A trusted hardware module in the dealer's server
A public random seed combined with the block hash

questions.explanation: SatvaShuffle generates a ZK-SNARK proof that the committed Merkle root corresponds to a valid permutation of a standard 52-card deck. Players can verify this proof without seeing the cards.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

SatvaShuffle generates a ZK-SNARK proof that the committed Merkle root corresponds to a valid permutation of a standard 52-card deck. Players can verify this proof without seeing the cards.

questions.text: How does Klethesia ensure that a slot machine's advertised RTP (e.g., 96.5%) is actually delivered to players?

options.text: The contract tracks cumulative bets and payouts; if actual RTP falls outside a statistical band, the contract can freeze and alert regulators
Each spin outcome is pre-determined by the operator and locked on-chain
A regulatory body manually reviews each spin
Players vote on whether the RTP was fair

questions.explanation: Klethesia enforces RTP statistically over a large number of spins. The smart contract monitors cumulative RTP and can take action (freezing payouts, flagging) if it deviates beyond the acceptable band, providing an automated, on-chain enforcement mechanism.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

Klethesia enforces RTP statistically over a large number of spins. The smart contract monitors cumulative RTP and can take action (freezing payouts, flagging) if it deviates beyond the acceptable band, providing an automated, on-chain enforcement mechanism.

questions.text: Why can no single validator bias the output of FortunaX?

options.text: The final output is an aggregate of threshold BLS signatures from multiple validators, and no single validator knows the final output until the threshold is reached
Validators are legally prohibited from manipulating randomness
The randomness is generated by a single trusted validator
FortunaX uses a centralised random number generator audited by MST

questions.explanation: FortunaX uses threshold cryptography: each validator contributes a share, and the final random number is the aggregate BLS signature. Until the threshold (⅔) is met, the output is unpredictable. A single validator cannot determine or bias the result.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

FortunaX uses threshold cryptography: each validator contributes a share, and the final random number is the aggregate BLS signature. Until the threshold (⅔) is met, the output is unpredictable. A single validator cannot determine or bias the result.

questions.text: In SatvaShuffle, why is the Merkle root of the shuffled deck published on-chain before any cards are dealt?

options.text: To cryptographically commit to the deck order so the dealer cannot change cards after seeing player actions
To allow players to see their opponents' cards in advance
To reduce the gas cost of dealing cards
To comply with data privacy regulations

questions.explanation: Publishing the Merkle root is a commitment scheme. Once committed, the dealer cannot alter the deck without invalidating the later reveal. This prevents the dealer from changing the shuffle after seeing bets or player actions.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

Publishing the Merkle root is a commitment scheme. Once committed, the dealer cannot alter the deck without invalidating the later reveal. This prevents the dealer from changing the shuffle after seeing bets or player actions.

questions.text: "On MST, game developers must write their own fairness algorithms - the blockchain does not provide any built-in gaming fairness protocols."
questions.explanation: MST provides three built-in fairness protocols: FortunaX (randomness), SatvaShuffle (card shuffling), and Klethesia (slot RTP). Game developers inherit fairness from the protocol layer; they do not need to implement their own.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Answer: FALSE

MST provides three built-in fairness protocols: FortunaX (randomness), SatvaShuffle (card shuffling), and Klethesia (slot RTP). Game developers inherit fairness from the protocol layer; they do not need to implement their own.

questions.text: "Klethesia guarantees that every individual spin of a slot machine pays out exactly the advertised RTP percentage."
questions.explanation:
1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.
                +1 mark: Explains that RTP is a statistical guarantee over many spins, not a per-spin guarantee, and mentions the epoch or band enforcement.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.
+1 mark: Explains that RTP is a statistical guarantee over many spins, not a per-spin guarantee, and mentions the epoch or band enforcement.

questions.text: In 100 - 150 words, explain why a player can trust the outcome of a rummy hand on MST using SatvaShuffle, even if the game operator is dishonest. Reference the commitment scheme and the ZK proof in your answer.
questions.explanation: Model Answer: A player can trust the outcome because SatvaShuffle uses a commitment scheme and ZK proofs to make cheating mathematically impossible. Before the hand, the operator publishes a Merkle root of the shuffled deck on-chain, cryptographically committing to the card order. A ZK-SNARK proof verifies that this root represents a valid permutation of a standard 52-card deck. During play, cards are revealed via Merkle proofs from that root - if the operator tried to change the deck, the proofs would fail. After the hand, the full deck is revealed and players can recompute the Merkle root to confirm no cards were added or removed. The operator cannot alter the shuffle after seeing player actions because the commitment is on-chain and irreversible.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Model Answer & Marks Breakdown

Model Answer: A player can trust the outcome because SatvaShuffle uses a commitment scheme and ZK proofs to make cheating mathematically impossible. Before the hand, the operator publishes a Merkle root of the shuffled deck on-chain, cryptographically committing to the card order. A ZK-SNARK proof verifies that this root represents a valid permutation of a standard 52-card deck. During play, cards are revealed via Merkle proofs from that root - if the operator tried to change the deck, the proofs would fail. After the hand, the full deck is revealed and players can recompute the Merkle root to confirm no cards were added or removed. The operator cannot alter the shuffle after seeing player actions because the commitment is on-chain and irreversible.

Marks breakdown (2 marks):
• 1 mark: Describes the commitment (Merkle root) and how it prevents post-hoc deck manipulation.
• 1 mark: Mentions the ZK proof of valid permutation and the final deck verification.
